sectionHeader.title: What is ADHD Coaching?
sectionHeader.subtitle: A thousand colorful bricks. One perplexing struggle.

subsections.title: Prologue: A View from the Rug

defaultContent.text:
You’re sitting on the floor, surrounded by a thousand colorful bricks.
You know exactly what you want to build — a 1,254-piece masterpiece. (Well, 1,251. You lost three pieces somewhere.)

You've wanted to build for weeks. You opened the box three times before.
Today is the day! But you don't start. You just sit there. Completely aware of everything you need to do, and yet completely unable to make yourself do it.

That gap, between knowing and doing, is one of the most disorienting parts of ADHD.
And it's exactly where coaching lives.

adhdContent.text: ⚡In an ADHD brain, the wiring between thinking and doing is frayed. Sometimes the spark carries, sometimes not, and rarely is the connection consistent.
⚡It's not a lack of willpower or intelligence. Certainly not laziness. It’s a bio-mechanical failure in the delivery system of your own intention.
⚡It’s frustrating. It's baffling. It’s invisible.

subsections.note: May wish to add a tooltip for bespoke
subsections.title: ADHD Coaching

defaultContent.text:
ADHD Coaching is a bespoke partnership.
Bespoke, because each person with ADHD experiences different challenges, and to arying extents. Coaching focuses on helping you understand how your brain works, and build strategies that work in accordance with it.
A coach is someone who sits beside you on the rug. Not to build it for you. Not to tell you how to build it. To help you figure out what will move you forward, and keep building.

Over time, as awareness and understanding deepen, you begin to notice and strategize by yourself. That shift — from coach-assisted to increasingly self-directed — is the whole point. The coaching relationship is designed to make itself less necessary over time.

adhdContent.text: ↔️ ADHD coaching closes the gap between wanting to and doing it — the defining frustration of ADHD.
🪡 It is a custom-tailored partnership because each ADHD brain actually works differently and presents different challenges.
🔍 Over time, coaching builds self-directed awareness — you start catching patterns earlier, needing less external scaffolding.

subsections.note: May wish to add a tooltip for reflective-inquiry
subsections.title: A Different Kind of Support

defaultContent.text: Coaching works alongside other forms of ADHD support — medication, therapy, productivity systems. It doesn't replace them.

Medication addresses the neurobiology directly. It clears the floor so you can begin to build. It doesn't teach you how to proceed next. Therapy works on emotional wounds and challenges that often travel alongside ADHD. Productivity systems often invite you to adapt your brain to someone else's methods. They tell you how to build, the way they did.

Traditional life coaching uses a similar reflective process — however, ADHD coaching is rooted in the neurobiology of the ADHD brain. It acknowledges the ADHD brain is fundamentally different in its operation of regions that affect motivation, focus, and self-regulation.

adhdContent.text: ◾Medication | Addresses neurobiology and enhances execution capability
◾Therapy | Explores the past and heals emotional wounds
◾Productivity tools | Often invite you to adapt to someone's else's system
◾ADHD Coaching | Grounded in brain science and focuses on execution.
◾Voodoo | Unclear approach. Mixed results.

subsections.note: Standard subsection — bullets in ADHD mode.
subsections.title: How Coaching Works

defaultContent.text:
Coaching is a conversation — a curious one.
At the start of the journey, there's excavation: mapping patterns, understanding your defaults, discovering strengths you draw on naturally.
Then much of the session lives in questions. This is the part that surprises people. A coach doesn't hand you an action plan or efficiency hacks. The exploration is the work.

Between sessions, there's usually one specific commitment — not a to-do list. Something small and specific enough to actually be attempted. And if you don't follow through, no guilt. Just data. You're not expected to get it right. You're expected to stay in the process.

adhdContent.text: The work happens through inquiry, not instructions
The coaching process surfaces your own insight; the coach doesn't hand you a plan
Small, specific commitments between sessions. No guilt for missed actions.

subsections.note: ADHD content is an image pair (TheirBuildOrder + MyBuildOrder2) — hardcoded in HTML. adhdContent here is ignored by the renderer. To edit the images or labels, update the HTML directly.
subsections.title: Adapt to the Way Your Brain Works

defaultContent.text:
"Make a list, and set a timer."
Much of the advice ADHD persons receive is built on the premise the problem is effort or organization. It isn't. It's a difference in neurobiology — specifically in the brain regions that govern activation: the ability to engage in tasks without immediate reward.

Research shows the ADHD brain thrives on Interest, Novelty, Challenge, Urgency, and Passion (abbreviated:INCUP). So, we can be strategic in how we approach a task — using brain science, instead of a barrel of caffeine.
You might struggle to follow linear instructions 1 through 12. But if tackling the most interesting part first is what gets you started — that's not cheating. That's strategy.

adhdContent.text: Strategically leverage INCUP: If you need to start with the fun parts first to stay interested, we do that.
The process must fit the person, not the other way around.

subsections.title: Self-Awareness + Self-Compassion Come First

defaultContent.text:
Most people arrive wanting to fix a problem. Or fix themselves. That's not where we start. We start with understanding.
Because when every mistake immediately becomes "what's wrong with me?" — you don't get insight. You get noise.

Self-awareness helps you stay curious about what's happening — even when what's happening is frustrating. Self-compassion lets you take risks with new strategies without the weight of judgment, and recover from inevitable setbacks with grace.

This isn't self-love in a bumper-sticker kind of way. It's motivation built on
earned self-respect.

adhdContent.text:
Things will inevitably go wrong sometimes.
Coaching supports you in replacing that inner critic with a curious observer

subsections.note: Standard subsection — emoji bullets in ADHD mode (emoji act as the visual marker, CSS hides the default LEGO stud).
subsections.title: When Coaching Makes Sense

defaultContent.text: Coaching makes sense when something isn't working and you're curious to explore further. Feeling like a mess is usually the honest starting point anyway.

It's also a natural fit at transition points:
◾ For adults: a new diagnosis, work, home, follow-through, or the private exhaustion of coping while feeling scattered underneath.
◾ For teens and young adults: school demands, building independence, finding routines that stick.
◾ For parents: seeking support for their child — and often, a better understanding of how to navigate this for themselves.
Coaching is a process, and it has limits. There will still be hard days. But with deeper understanding, life tends to feel more workable. Not effortless. But lighter.

adhdContent.text: ✅ Readiness isn’t about having your life together. It's simply being curious enough to try a different approach.
✅ For adults, teens, and parents, it’s often where the frustration finally has somewhere useful to go.
❌ If you're in acute crisis, therapy is likely the better first step.
